name: ragged coat
shortDescription: A ragged coat has more repairs than dignity, but it still keeps wind off a tired back. Its torn hem and tired seams speak of bad weather, hard roads, and owners who valued warmth over appearances.
flavorText: “A ragged coat is poverty that refused to freeze politely.”

name: royal coat
shortDescription: A royal coat is cut for ceremony, rich fabric, and the posture of someone used to being watched. Even folded on a stall, it carries the stiff confidence of court halls, polished floors, and servants who know not to brush against it.
flavorText: “A royal coat does not keep out cold so much as remind cold of its station.”

name: thick coat
shortDescription: A thick coat is honest winter gear, heavy with wool, padding, and the promise of surviving bad roads after sunset. It lacks elegance, but anyone who has crossed a frozen bridge knows elegance is often just warmth that has never been tested.
flavorText: “A thick coat wins arguments no tailor ever hears.”

name: black dress
shortDescription: A black dress is a versatile garment of mourning, formality, restraint, and quiet drama. Its value depends on cut and cloth, but even a simple one can make a wearer look as though they know more than they plan to say.
flavorText: “Black cloth is where elegance goes when it wants fewer witnesses.”

name: corset
shortDescription: A corset is shaped discipline: laces, stays, pressure, posture, and the uneasy bargain between fashion and breathing. Good work supports the wearer; bad work only supports the tailor’s reputation for cruelty.
flavorText: “A corset teaches the body what society already decided.”

name: light blue dress
shortDescription: A light blue dress has the clean, airy look of clear mornings, spring visits, and careful innocence. It is popular for festivals and family introductions, where softness must be visible but not too eager.
flavorText: “Light blue is the color modesty chooses when white feels too certain.”

name: scarce red dress
shortDescription: A scarce red dress is bold fabric made for entrances, exits, and the dangerous space between them. Its color turns candlelight greedy and makes even quiet wearers look like they arrived with a decision already made.
flavorText: “A red dress is not worn to disappear.”

name: white dress
shortDescription: A white dress is simple only until weather, wine, road dust, or gossip gets involved. It suggests purity, ceremony, and expense, because keeping white cloth clean is its own public announcement.
flavorText: “White dresses are promises made before mud has its say.”

name: ceremonial gloves
shortDescription: Ceremonial gloves are clean, fitted, and meant for hands that will point, bless, sign, or receive tribute without touching anything unpleasant. They are less about warmth than distance, which is often the truest luxury.
flavorText: “Ceremony begins when even the hands are dressed for witnesses.”

name: rough gloves
shortDescription: Rough gloves are patched workwear for hauling rope, splitting wood, turning soil, or grabbing things better left unnamed. Their leather is stiff, scarred, and dependable in the way pretty gloves rarely survive long enough to become.
flavorText: “Rough gloves remember every splinter their owner forgot to thank them for.”

name: fancy gloves
shortDescription: Fancy gloves are smooth, careful, and made for gestures rather than labor. They suit dances, courts, card tables, and people who believe a clean hand can hide a dirty arrangement.
flavorText: “Fancy gloves make even a refusal look rehearsed.”

name: fencer gloves
shortDescription: Fencer gloves are light, close-fitting protection for quick wrists and quicker pride. They are made to grip steel, soften mistakes, and let a duelist pretend the hand was never shaking.
flavorText: “A fencer’s glove knows the difference between courage and timing.”

name: working gloves
shortDescription: Working gloves are plain tools for hands that cannot afford tenderness. They gather oil, dust, sweat, resin, and every other proof that something useful happened before sunset.
flavorText: “Working gloves are the ledger of labor, written in stains.”

name: straw hat
shortDescription: A straw hat is light field armor against sun, dust, and the long cruelty of open noon. Farmers, fishers, and road peddlers favor it because shade is cheaper than courage and more reliable than clouds.
flavorText: “A straw hat is a small roof for a moving life.”

name: bear cap
shortDescription: A bear cap is warm, shaggy, and slightly ridiculous until the wind turns cruel. It carries a hunter’s boast on the head and enough fur to make winter seem personally insulted.
flavorText: “Wear a bear cap and every tavern asks which of you won.”

name: brown cap
shortDescription: A brown cap is ordinary headwear for ordinary weather, which makes it useful in nearly every town. It hides dust, travels well, and asks for no attention beyond the small mercy of keeping a skull covered.
flavorText: “A brown cap is humble because it already knows it will be worn the most.”

name: rough cap
shortDescription: A rough cap is a cheap, patched thing made for work yards, cold mornings, and owners who misplace better clothing. Its value is not beauty but readiness, especially when rain arrives before pride wakes up.
flavorText: “A rough cap has never looked noble, but it has saved many ears.”

name: starcourt leather helm
shortDescription: A Starcourt leather helm is shaped for guards who need agility, polish, and a little visible allegiance. The leather is worked cleanly, often stamped or trimmed to show service under a banner rather than a back-alley brawl.
flavorText: “Starcourt helms shine just enough to remind trouble it is being observed.”

name: fancy veil
shortDescription: A fancy veil is fine cloth for hiding, revealing, and turning both acts into etiquette. It softens the face, sharpens curiosity, and lets the wearer decide how much of the room deserves an answer.
flavorText: “A veil is not secrecy; it is negotiation in fabric.”

name: fuzzy cap
shortDescription: A fuzzy cap is warm, soft, and cheerful enough to forgive its lack of dignity. It sells best when weather turns sharp and people remember that comfort has never needed to look serious.
flavorText: “A fuzzy cap is what practicality wears when it wants to be liked.”

name: jester cap
shortDescription: A jester cap is bright, pointed, bell-happy nonsense designed to make attention unavoidable. It is costume, warning, and license all at once, because fools are often the only people allowed to say the obvious safely.
flavorText: “The bells laugh first so the jester can survive second.”

name: mysterious veil
shortDescription: A mysterious veil is darker, heavier, and less decorative than a courtly one, made to turn a face into a question. Buyers seek it for rituals, disguises, mourning, or dramatic entrances they will later pretend were accidental.
flavorText: “A mysterious veil is a door that walks.”

name: peasant cap
shortDescription: A peasant cap is simple cloth headwear for fields, kitchens, workshops, and errands that begin before sunrise. It is cheap, familiar, and so common that only a fool would underestimate what passes beneath it.
flavorText: “Peasant caps have heard more truth than crowns.”

name: coif
shortDescription: A coif is close cloth for covering the head beneath helm, habit, or household rule. It is practical, modest, and easy to overlook, which is precisely why it appears in so many lives and so few portraits.
flavorText: “A coif is the quiet foundation beneath louder clothing.”

name: wimple
shortDescription: A wimple frames the face with solemn cloth, often worn for devotion, station, or the disciplined refusal of vanity. It speaks softly but firmly, like a door closing on frivolous conversation.
flavorText: “A wimple does not hide the face; it edits the room’s expectations.”

name: butterfly mask
shortDescription: A butterfly mask spreads bright wings across the eyes, turning the wearer into festival color and polite mystery. It is favored at masquerades where everyone wants to be recognized just enough to be remembered.
flavorText: “A butterfly mask lets a person molt without leaving the ballroom.”

name: ghost mask
shortDescription: A ghost mask is pale, hollow-eyed, and meant to make silence feel crowded. Actors, mourners, pranksters, and ritual keepers all use it differently, but every buyer checks the inside before trying it on.
flavorText: “A ghost mask is only costume until someone answers to it.”

name: cyclopse mask
shortDescription: A cyclopse mask fixes one large eye where two should be, making the wearer look blunt, watchful, and faintly cursed. It is popular in monster plays and tavern dares, especially among people who spell accuracy less carefully than fear.
flavorText: “One eye is enough when the point is being noticed.”

name: devil mask
shortDescription: A devil mask wears horns, grin, and accusation in equal measure. It belongs to festivals, threats, morality plays, and anyone who wants to borrow wickedness without paying the full spiritual cost.
flavorText: “The devil mask smiles because someone else has to remove it.”

name: goofy mask
shortDescription: A goofy mask is foolish on purpose, with exaggerated features made to disarm crowds and humiliate dignity. It is cheap fun, unless worn at the wrong negotiation, where it becomes expensive very quickly.
flavorText: “A goofy mask proves pride is easier to damage than pottery.”

name: venetian mask
shortDescription: A Venetian mask is smooth, elegant, and designed for secrets that still want candlelight. Its polished surface turns identity into an accessory, which is why merchants sell them near both theaters and scandals.
flavorText: “A Venetian mask hides the face while improving the rumor.”

name: monster mask
shortDescription: A monster mask is broad, toothy, and wonderfully unsubtle, made for frightening children, amusing adults, or confusing guards at poor distance. Its craftsmanship matters less than the first startled step backward.
flavorText: “A monster mask is successful if someone drops a cup.”

name: skull mask
shortDescription: A skull mask makes mortality wearable, which says something uncomfortable about fashion. It is used in funerary plays, grave festivals, robber disguises, and philosophical parties that never improve the wine.
flavorText: “The skull mask is the one face every face is practicing.”

name: ash fiend mask
shortDescription: An ash fiend mask is soot-dark, sharp-featured, and made to suggest something crawled out of a burned shrine with opinions. It is prized by fire cults, actors, and anyone who wants terror without hiring the actual fiend.
flavorText: “Ash fiends leave marks; the mask merely borrows the warning.”

name: animal skin rags
shortDescription: Animal skin rags are rough hide scraps stitched or tied into clothing by necessity rather than design. They smell of smoke, rain, and survival, and they keep a body covered when better cloth belongs to luckier people.
flavorText: “Rags made from hide remember the beast and the poverty.”

name: scarce big cat skin rags
shortDescription: Scarce big cat skin rags carry spotted or striped remnants of a predator that once moved with more grace than its current owner likely does. They are rough, rare, and faintly boastful, even when worn by someone pretending not to boast.
flavorText: “Big cat rags say poverty met danger and kept a souvenir.”

name: strong leather rags
shortDescription: Strong leather rags are patched from durable hide, ugly but far harder to tear than their name suggests. Travelers and laborers prize them because survival often arrives badly dressed.
flavorText: “Strong leather rags are what armor becomes after losing its manners.”

name: enchanted rags
shortDescription: Enchanted rags look worthless until they refuse rain, mend a tear, or sit strangely warm on a cold night. Their magic is not grand, but the poor have always known small miracles are the ones that matter.
flavorText: “Never mock enchanted rags; they may be dressed better than you in ways you cannot see.”

name: fabled Unraveling rags
shortDescription: Fabled Unraveling rags hang loose and frayed, yet every thread seems to know more than cloth should. They are tied to old undoing charms, broken disguises, and stories where the poorest garment survives the richest lie.
flavorText: “Unraveling rags do not fall apart; they invite other things to join them.”

name: scarce blue robes
shortDescription: Scarce blue robes are dyed in a deep, costly shade favored by scholars, judges, and quiet magicians. The cut is usually simple, letting the color do the expensive work of suggesting calm authority.
flavorText: “Blue robes make wisdom look like it arrived on purpose.”

name: rough brown robes
shortDescription: Rough brown robes are plain traveler’s cloth for monks, hedge-wizards, poor scribes, and anyone who expects dust to win eventually. They are not impressive, but they forgive hard roads better than fine fabric ever could.
flavorText: “Brown robes do not promise holiness; they promise the stains will blend.”

name: fabled robes of Unraveling
shortDescription: Fabled robes of Unraveling move with an uneasy looseness, their seams seeming to question the idea of staying whole. Sorcerers fear and desire them in equal measure, because a garment that loosens bindings may not stop at thread.
flavorText: “Wear the robes of Unraveling only if you trust what holds you together.”

name: flashy robes
shortDescription: Flashy robes are bright, dramatic, and allergic to subtlety. They suit stage mages, wealthy eccentrics, and anyone convinced that if magic is worth doing, the sleeves should announce it first.
flavorText: “Flashy robes are half garment, half advertisement.”

name: fine green robes
shortDescription: Fine green robes are soft, well-dyed, and suggestive of gardens, money, and controlled good taste. They are favored by healers, stewards, and court scholars who want gentleness to look well funded.
flavorText: “Fine green robes make restraint look fertile.”

name: long black robes
shortDescription: Long black robes trail with solemn authority, swallowing light around the hem and sharpening every gesture. They are beloved by judges, mourners, cultists, and dramatic academics, which makes provenance unusually important.
flavorText: “Long black robes make even walking away seem like a verdict.”

name: long purple robes
shortDescription: Long purple robes carry old royal color in generous folds, giving the wearer a borrowed sense of consequence. Whether priest, mage, or noble pretender, anyone wearing them must be ready for people to expect speeches.
flavorText: “Purple robes are heavy with all the importance they imply.”

name: silk robes
shortDescription: Silk robes are smooth, cool, and costly enough to make comfort feel suspiciously political. They rustle softly, catch light beautifully, and remind every sensible merchant that delicacy can be very expensive.
flavorText: “Silk robes whisper because they know everyone is listening.”

name: nightstar robes
shortDescription: Nightstar robes are dark cloth pricked with pale points, like a private sky folded around the shoulders. Astrologers, dream-readers, and ambitious apprentices prize them for making uncertainty look ordained.
flavorText: “Nightstar robes are for those who want the heavens close enough to wear.”

name: red robes
shortDescription: Red robes are warm, commanding, and difficult to ignore in any hall smaller than a throne room. They suit fire-priests, bold mages, festival speakers, and people who consider restraint a failure of tailoring.
flavorText: “Red robes do not enter quietly; they arrive already declared.”

name: teal robes
shortDescription: Teal robes balance green and blue in a way that feels watery, thoughtful, and slightly foreign to plain village dye vats. They are favored by coastal scholars, river priests, and merchants who enjoy a color nobody names the same way twice.
flavorText: “Teal robes are what water would wear if asked to lecture.”
